coredata.dc:title: Bright orange-emissive carbon quantum dots as an ultrasensitive nanoprobe for new coccine determination in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yikun
Yang, Zhen-quan
Xiao, Lixia
Yang, Ming
Gong, Xiaojuan
Liu, Lizhen
Han, Jie
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an ultrasensitive fluorescence method based on bright orange-emissive carbon quantum dots (OE-CQDs) was for the first time developed for the detection of new coccine (NC). The OE-CQDs were synthesised by a one-pot solvothermal method with saffron T as precursor. The detection mechanism was based on the interaction between OE-CQDs and NC, where the fluorescence of OE-CQDs was inhibited by NC via inner filter effect (IFE) and static interaction. The OE-CQDs probe exhibited high selectivity and good anti-interference ability towards NC sensing. A linear relationship with the concentration of NC in a range of 0.01–20.0 µmol L−1 and a detection limit as low as 1.8 nmol L−1 were obtained. The developed fluorescence method was successfully applied for NC determination in commercial food samples, including dried waxberry, haw flakes, watermelon frost lozenges, strawberry jam and energy drink, with satisfactory results were achieved. The recoveries were in a range of 97.0–104.0% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.74%. This work highlights the great potential of using long-wavelength emissive CQDs as nanoprobe to achieve enhanced sensitivity in food additives sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: OE-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching
Large stokes shifts
NC
Inner filter effect
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: NC determination in food samples by OE-CQDs-based fluorescence method
NC determination in food samples by HPLC Analysis
Optimisation of the parameters for NC sensing work
Fluorescence quenching of OE-CQDs by NC
The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the OE-CQDs-based sensing platform
The fluorescence quenching mechanism of OE-CQDs by NC
Determination of NC contents in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image and (B) the particle size distribution histogram of the as-synthesised OE-CQDs. (C) The FTIR and (D) the XPS spectrum of OE-CQDs.

(A) The UV–vis absorption (red curve), excitation (blue curve) and emission (black curve) spectra of OE-CQDs. (B) The PL spectra of OE-CQDs at various λex. The inset is the photograph of OE-CQDs solution under UV irradiation at 365 nm.

(A) The effect of OE-CQDs dosage on F0/F. The OE-CQDs solutions were prepared in ultrapure water at a concentration range of 0.003–0.1 mg mL−1. (B) The effect of pH on F0/F. The OE-CQDs solutions were prepared in PBS solutions at pH 2.0–11.0 and ultrapure water (pH 5.8) with OE-CQDs concentrations setting at 0.005 mg mL−1. (C) The effect of reaction time on F0/F. The OE-CQDs solution was prepared in ultrapure water at a concentration of 0.005 mg mL−1. For all the experiments, the amount of NC added was 20.0 µmol L−1.

(A) The PL spectra of a OE-CQDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.005 mg mL−1) after the addition of various amounts of NC (1−15): 0.0, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 12.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 30.0, 35.0, 40.0, 45.0, 50.0, 60.0, 75.0 µmol L−1). The inset is the photograph of OE-CQDs solution before and after the addition of NC under UV irradiation at 365 nm. (B) The SV plots of F0/F against the NC content. The error bars indicate the standard deviations from five independent measurements (n = 5).

(A) The F0/F of OE-CQDs solution (0.005 mg mL−1) in the presence of NC (20.0 µmol L−1) and various possible interfering substances (20.0 µmol L−1). (B) The F0/F of the OE-CQDs solution (0.005 mg mL−1)/NC (20.0 µmol L−1) mixture in the absence and in the presence of other potential interfering species. The error bars are the standard deviations from five independent experiments (n = 5).

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue curve) of NC and the excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve) spectra of OE-CQDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curves of an OE-CQDs solution (0.005 mg mL−1) in the absence and in the presence of 20.0 µmol L−1 NC.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of OE-CQDs and the principle for NC detection.
Comparison of the performance of different analytical methods for NC detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HPLC-UV-DIODE: high performance liquid chromatography coupled to UV diode array.
HPLC-DAD: high performance liquid chromatography-diode array detection.
N,P-OE-CQDs: nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for NC detection in food samples using the proposed OE-CQDs-based analytic platform and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Bright orange-emissive carbon quantum dots as an ultrasensitive nanoprobe for new coccine determination in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an ultrasensitive fluorescence method based on bright orange-emissive carbon quantum dots (OE-CQDs) was for the first time developed for the detection of new coccine (NC). The OE-CQDs were synthesised by a one-pot solvothermal method with saffron T as precursor. The detection mechanism was based on the interaction between OE-CQDs and NC, where the fluorescence of OE-CQDs was inhibited by NC via inner filter effect (IFE) and static interaction. The OE-CQDs probe exhibited high selectivity and good anti-interference ability towards NC sensing. A linear relationship with the concentration of NC in a range of 0.01–20.0 µmol L−1 and a detection limit as low as 1.8 nmol L−1 were obtained. The developed fluorescence method was successfully applied for NC determination in commercial food samples, including dried waxberry, haw flakes, watermelon frost lozenges, strawberry jam and energy drink, with satisfactory results were achieved. The recoveries were in a range of 97.0–104.0% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.74%. This work highlights the great potential of using long-wavelength emissive CQDs as nanoprobe to achieve enhanced sensitivity in food additives sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic food colorants with the merits of low cost and chemical stability have been widely used in food industry to beautify foods colour (Hu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2015). New coccine (NC, also called as: Ponceau 4 R; E124; Acid red 18) is one of the commonly used synthetic food colorants, which belongs to the group of azo dye with an appearance of reddish powder (Ma et al., 2018; Thiam et al., 2015). Using NC as food additive makes the foods attractive in appearance. However, the acute (mice and rats) and short-term (rat) toxicity studies showed that NC has low levels of oral toxicity and will elicit immune response, which would certainly threaten human health (Gaunt et al., 1967; Ibero et al., 1982).

In response to the potential hazard of NC, many counties have made strict rules on using NC as food colorant. The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and World Health Organization (WHO) Joint Expert Committee on Food Additives (JECFA, 2019) established a 0–4 mg kg 1 body weight value as the acceptable daily intake (ADI) of NC. Some countries like the United States, Denmark, Norway and Canada have strictly prohibited the use of NC as food additive (Cyriac et al., 2018). In many countries, it is permitted as an additive to certain foods and the amount is strictly limited (Xie et al., 2012). For instance, China government allow NC to be used in processing food like candies, cakes, syrups, jams, beverages, cans, frozen drinks and preserves with a maximum permitted content of 0.05–0.5 g kg 1 (GB 2760–2014). However, the excessive usage of NC in foods still happens from time to time (Chen et al., 2017; Cyriac et al., 2018; Iammarino et al., 2020). In order to ensure the safety of consumers, it is necessary to develop an efficient, sensitive, and accurate analytical method for NC detection.

So far, a variety of analytical methods have been developed for NC detection, including high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) (Kim et al., 2018), reverse phase HPLC (RP-HPLC) (Li et al., 2020; Sulekova et al., 2016), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Wu et al., 2020) and voltammetry (Chanlon et al., 2005; Cheng et al., 2015; de Moraes et al., 2018). However, most of the above detection methods suffer from time-consuming, expensive instruments and/or cumbersome sample pre-treatment. Recent studies showed that the spectrophotometric methods (Meral and Elik, 2021; Turak et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2019), especially the fluorescence nanoprobe-based spectrophotometry is an efficient analytical technique for food colorants detection with outstanding merits of simplicity, short response time, easy operation, good selectivity and high sensitivity (Liu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020a; Zhou et al., 2019).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are dispersed spherical-like carbon particles with sizes below 10 nm (Vieira et al., 2017). The CQDs were reported to have extraordinary tenable fluorescence properties, surface functional plasticity, chemical stability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility (Jiang et al., 2015; Tsai et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020b). With these remarkable physical and chemical properties, the use of CQDs as fluorescence nanoprobe for food additives detection have become research hot spots, especially their usage for food colorants (such as sunset yellow, tartrazine, indigo carmine and curcumin) (Ali et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2016) detection. The mechanisms of CQDs-based sensing systems for food colorants detection usually include inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), where the former one could provide higher sensitivity than the later one since the changes of a fluorophore absorbance in a IFE sensing system can exponentially transform into the changes of fluorescence intensity (Hu et al., 2019). In order to establish a IFE sensing system with high sensitivity, continuous efforts have been made to tune the structure and fluorescence property of CQDs. Recently, it was reported that the use of CQDs with long-wavelength emission as nanoprobe works efficiently in improving the detection selectivity and sensitivity in comparison to the widely-investigated blue-emitting CQDs which is susceptible to interference from lack of large Stokes shifts or scattering spectra of solvent (Wang et al., 2020). So far, even though several studies showed that the potential of CQDs-based nanoprobe for NC detection (Gunjal et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). However, the CQDs involved were short-wavelength blue emissive, which limits the analytical performance of CQDs-based nanoprobe. As such, the exploration of efficient long-wavelength emissive CQDs probe to construct an efficient IFE sensing system for NC detection is in great demand.

This work proposes the use of bright orange-emissive carbon quantum dots (OE-CQDs) to establish an efficient NC sensing system for the purpose of achieving enhanced analytical performance ( Scheme 1). The OE-CQDs was prepared by a one-pot solvothermal method with saffron T as starting material. The fluorescence of OE-CQDs can be efficiently quenched by NC via inner filter effect (IFE) and static interaction. Inspired by this phenomenon, an OE-CQDs-based fluorescence nanoprobe was proposed for NC detection. The sensing conditions including OE-CQDs dosage, pH and reaction time, were investigated in detail. The analytical method was thoroughly validated by evaluating its linearity, limit of detection, selectivity and anti-interference ability. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Finally, the proposed fluorescence method was used to quantify NC in real food commodities. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time to use long-wavelength emissive CQDs to establish an IFE sensing system for the purpose of achieving enhanced analytical performance in NC sensing.

All reagents and chemicals used in this work are of analytical grade or above and were used without further treatment. Safranine T, ethanol, rhodamine B dye, glucose, fructose, sucrose, glutathione and all amino acids were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The other chemicals were all gained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water used for solution preparation was purified by a Millipore Milli-Q Plus system (Bedford, MA, USA). The phosphate buffer solution (PBS) solutions at 10.0 mmol L−1 were adjusted with 10.0 mmol L−1 Na3PO4, Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4.

The OE-CQDs was synthesised according to a previously reported method (Meng et al., 2021) with slight modification. At first, 0.25 g of safranine T was added to 20.0 mL of ethanol and subjected to ultra-sonication to assist dissolve. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature, the obtained product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was collected and air-dried at room temperature to volatilise the residual ethanol. Finally, the purified OE-CQDs solution was freeze-dried for further use.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of OE-CQDs was measured with a CM100 electron microscope (Philips, Eindhoven, Netherlands). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) measurement was studied by a Varian 670-IR + 610-IR FTIR spectrometer (Lincolnshire, IL, USA). The x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed by an ESCALAB 250 x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The UV−vis spectrum was acquired on a UV-2550 absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The elemental analysis was obtained on a vario EL cube organic elemental analyser (Elementar, Hanau, Germany). The PL spectrum of OE-CQDs was investigated by a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence decay measurements were acquired on A FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Co., Ltd., England).

In order to study the linearity of the proposed fluorescence method, the OE-CQDs product was dissolved in ultrapure water to obtain an OE-CQDs solution (0.005 mg mL−1). Unless otherwise stated, the OE-CQDs solutions were prepared in ultrapure water. Then, different concentrations of NC were gradually added into a 2.0 mL OE-CQDs solution, making the added NC in a concentration range of 0.0–75.0 μmol L−1. The fluorescence intensities of the OE-CQDs solution containing various NC contents were recorded at 590 nm (excitation wavelength (λex) = 473 nm). The 3σ/slope rule was used to determine the detection limit, where σ was calculated with twelve repeated measurements of an OE-CQDs blank solution.

The selectivity of the proposed fluorescence method was investigated by monitoring the response of the OE-CQDs fluorescence intensities towards NC and various interfering species, including 6 metal ions (Ca2+, K+, Na+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Ba2+), 10 anionic ions (Cl−, F−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, C2O4 2−, CH3COO−, HPO4 2− and H2PO4 −), 11 amino acids (DL-methionine, β-hydroxyalanine, arginine, threonine, histidine, L-methionine, glycine, alanine, leucine, glutathiol and glutathione), some small molecules (folic acid, sucrose, glucose and fructose) and other similar food colorants (metanil yellow, indigo, amaranth, sudan Ⅰ and auramine O). In detail, 2.0 μL of aqueous solution of NC (20.0 mmol L−1) and the above mentioned interfering substances (20.0 mmol L−1) were added into a 2.0 mL OE-CQDs solution (0.005 mg mL−1), respectively, making the added contents of each substance at a concentration level of 20.0 µmol L−1. The fluorescence intensities of OE-CQDs before and after adding the above substances were measured at 590 nm (λex=473 nm).

The anti-interference ability of the proposed method was measured by evaluating the fluorescence response of an OE-CQDs/NC mixture solution to the above mentioned potential interferences. In detail, 2.0 μL of an aqueous solution of NC (20.0 μmol L−1) was firstly added into a 2.0 mL OE-CQDs solution (0.005 mg mL−1), and then the above mentioned interfering species were added into the mixture solutions with the concentration of each interferences setting at 20.0 µmol L−1. The fluorescence intensities of the OE-CQDs/NC system before and after mixing with the above possible interferences were measured at 590 nm (λex=473 nm).

Five kinds of commercial food samples, including dried waxberry, haw flakes, watermelon frost lozenges, strawberry jam and energy drink, were purchased from the local supermarket at Yangzhou City, China. The samples were treated according to a previously reported method with slight modification (Hu et al., 2021a). In brief, the solid samples were grinded into fine powder. 3.0 g of them were weighed and dissolved in 10.0 mL of ultrapure water, and the NC was extracted with the assistance of ultra-sonication for 10 min. Then, the supernatant was collected after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The liquid samples were diluted with ultrapure water (1:5, v/v) before measurement. All the sample extractions were filtered through a cellulose acetate membrane (0.22 µm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NC determination in food samples by OE-CQDs-based fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: For the NC content quantification in the above-mentioned food samples, 20.0 μL of the extraction of each food sample was added into a 2.0 mL OE-CQDs solution (0.005 mg mL−1), respectively. The fluorescence intensities of OE-CQDs were recorded at 590 nm (λex=473 nm) before and after the addition of sample extractions. The recoveries of quantification were investigated to confirm the accuracy of the proposed method. In detail, three concentration levels of NC were spiked into the extractions of each food sample with the final concentrations at 1.0, 3.0 and 5.0 µmol L−1, respectively. Then, the contents of NC in spiked sample extraction liquids were determined by the proposed fluorescence method in five replicate assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NC determination in food samples by HPLC Analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of NC in food samples were also determined by HPLC. The measurement was carried out on a 1200 series Agilent HPLC (Santa Clara, CA, USA) with a photodiode array detector (PAD). The chromatographic separation was carried out by using a C18 column (150 ×4.6 mm id stainless steel) from Waters Corporation (Milford, MA, USA). The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 10.0 mmol L−1 ammonium acetate (90:10, v/v). The flow rate was set as 1.0 mL min−1 and the injection volume was 20.0 μL. The UV detection was monitored at 508 nm. Three replicate assays were performed for each measurement.

The morphology and size distribution information of OE-CQDs were characterised by TEM and the corresponding TEM image was displayed in Fig. 1 A. It can be seen that the as-prepared OE-CQDs species were spherical in structure and were well dispersed on the TEM grid. The histogram of particle size distribution (Fig. 1B) was obtained by counting the approximately 100 particles on various spots of the TEM image. The sizes of the OE-CQDs were revealed to disperse in a range of 0.75 − 4.25 nm with an average particle size of 1.75 ± 0.5 nm.

The elemental composition of the OE-CQDs was studied by elemental analysis. As shown in Table S1, the OE-CQDs were composed of C 59.71%, H 5.949%, O 10.505%, N 13.223% and Cl 10.613%. The FTIR and XPS spectroscopy were used to characterise the surface functional groups of the OE-CQDs. As shown by FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1 C, the absorption peaks at 3409 cm−1 and 3191 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O−H and N − H (Hu et al., 2021b), respectively. The absorption peak at 2923 cm−1 was corresponding to the stretching vibration of C–H (Sun et al., 2019). The absorption band at 1610 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of CO bond (Lu et al., 2019). The peaks at 1531 cm−1 and 1494 cm−1 were ascribed to CC vibration (Ma et al., 2019). The absorption peak at 1334 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N (Wang et al., 2018). The full scan XPS spectrum of OE-CQDs (Fig. 3D) showed three main predominant peaks at 284.1, 398.1 and 531.1 eV, attributing to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S1A) can be fitted into three obvious characteristic peaks at 284.1, 286.1 and 288.3 eV of sp2 carbons (C–C/CC), sp3 carbons (C−O/C−N) and carboxyl carbons (COOH), respectively (Farwa et al., 2018; He et al., 2020). The N1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) showed the existence of amino-N at 399.3 eV and pyrrolic-N at 400.8 eV (Deng et al., 2019). The O1s high resolution spectra (Fig. S1C) can be resolved into two doublets of CO (532.0 eV) and C–O (533.4 eV) (Meng et al., 2021). The results are consistent with FTIR spectroscopic analysis, illustrating that the OE-CQDs surface had numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl functionalities, which imparts the OE-CQDs good hydrophilicity.

To study the optical properties of OE-CQDs, the UV−vis absorption and PL spectra of the OE-CQDs were acquired. As seen from the UV−vis absorption spectrum of OE-CQDs (red curve, Fig. 2 A), the OE-CQDs had three typical absorption peaks at 214, 228 and 349 nm. The former two were attributed to π-π * transition of the unsaturated aromatic ring (Lu et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2019c) and the later one was ascribed to n–π * transition of C–O bond (Lu et al., 2017). The excitation spectrum of OE-CQDs (blue curve, Fig. 2 A) showed that the OE-CQDs had three typical excitation peaks at 302, 473 and 546 nm, among which 473 nm was the optimal one. When excited at 473 nm, the OE-CQDs showed a long-wavelength emission peak at 590 nm (black curve, Fig. 2 A), which affords OE-CQDs the possibility with high sensitivity in serving as nanoprobe for NC detection. The effect of ionic strength on CQDs fluorescence intensity was studied. As shown by Fig. S2, no meaningful increase or reduction in CQDs fluorescence intensity was observed in a wide ionic strength range (0.0–1.0 M NaCl). Fig. 2B displayed the emission peaks of OE-CQDs at different λex. It can be seen that the OE-CQDs showed excitation-independent behaviour with no obvious shift of the emission bands as the λex changed from 410 to 560 nm. The phenomenon was also observed in previous studies, which indicated that the OE-CQDs were homogeneous in size (Liu et al., 2017; Wen and Yin, 2016). It was observed that the OE-CQDs solution emitted intense orange colour under UV irradiation (Inset of Fig. 2 B). The quantum yield of OE-CQDs (Fig. S3) was calculated to be 5.08% by using rhodamine 6 G as reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimisation of the parameters for NC sensing work
ce:section.ce:para: Since the working conditions play an important role in governing the analytical performance of the proposed fluorescence method, the studies were firstly given to evaluate the effect of working parameters, including OE-CQDs dosage, pH and reaction time, on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of OE-CQDs induced by NC, i.e., F0/F, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of OE-CQDs before and after the introduction of NC, respectively.

Fig. 3 A displayed the changes of F0/F with the dosage of OE-CQDs (0.003–0.1 mg mL−1). It was observed that the highest F0/F was obtained when the concentration of OE-CQDs was 0.005 mg mL−1 and therefore, 0.005 mg mL−1 OE-CQDs was used as the optimum dosage for the subsequent sensing work. The F0/F at various pH were evaluated to study the effect of pH. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the F0/F did not change significantly when pH changed from 2.0 to 11.0 (all pH environments were provided by 10.0 mmol L−1 PBS except pH 5.8 which was provided by ultrapure water). To simplify the sensing work, pH 5.8 was selected as the optimal pH and ultrapure water was used to prepare OE-CQDs working solutions. The optimal response time was evaluated by observing the change of F0/F as a function of reaction time. As shown in Fig. 3 C, the F0/F sharply increased within 1.0 min after the OE-CQDs was mixed with NC and remained stable afterwards. As such, 1.0 min was used as the optimal reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of OE-CQDs by NC
ce:section.ce:para:
With the superior optical properties, OE-CQDs were used as nanoprobe for NC detection. The effect of NC concentration on the fluorescence intensities of OE-CQDs was investigated under the optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensities of OE-CQDs gradually declined as the concentration of NC increased. The relationship between F0/F and NC content exhibited great linearity in a concentration range of 0.01–20.0 µmol L−1 (Fig. 4B). The concentration-dependent of F0/F was fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F=1 +K
SV [NC], where [NC] and K
SV are the concentration of NC and the Stern-Volmer constant, respectively). The K
SV was calculated to be 0.516 × 105 L mol−1 with a determination correlation R
2 of 0.9994. The detection limit estimated according to the 3σ (signal-to-noise ratio) rule was calculated to be 1.8 nmol L−1.

The analytical performance of this method was compared with the existing methods. As shown in Table 1, the detection limit of this method was 1–2 orders of magnitude lower than any of the existing blue emissive CQDs-based fluorescence methods (Gunjal et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022), and it was even much lower than the previously reported HPLC (Donmez et al., 2020; Iammarino et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020), voltammetry (Qin et al., 2020) and the other spectrophotometry methods (Demirhan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2017). As proof of concept demonstration, our work on using long-wavelength emissive OE-CQDs achieved remarkable improvement on LOD. Apart from high sensitivity feature, this proposed method showed additional advantages of easy operation, fast detection and low cost.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the OE-CQDs-based sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Good selectivity and anti-interference ability are significantly important for a fluorescence sensing platform. To evaluate the selective characteristics of this proposed method, the fluorescence responses of OE-CQDs to NC and various possible interferences, including different kinds of metal ions, anions, amino acids, small molecules and food colorants, were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, only NC obviously suppressed the fluorescence intensities of OE-CQDs while the other possible interfering substances had almost negligible effects on the fluorescence intensities of OE-CQDs, indicating that OE-CQDs have high selectivity for NC sensing. Subsequently, the anti-interference ability of OE-CQDs was studied by adding the possible interfering substances to an OE-CQDs (0.005 mg mL−1)/NC (20.0 µmol L−1) mixture solution, where the concentration of other possible interfering substances was 20.0 µmol L−1. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensities of the OE-CQDs/NC sensing system did not change significantly after mixing with the above possible interfering substances, indicating that the OE-CQDs/NC sensing system had high anti-interference ability. The above experimental results showed that the proposed sensing system had excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability in detecting NC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of OE-CQDs by NC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to the study the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the spectral properties of NC and OE-CQDs were firstly studied if the popular IFE mechanism exists, which would occur if the absorption band of an absorber overlaps to some extent with the excitation and/or emission band of a fluorophore (Yang et al., 2019b). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NC (blue curve) and the excitation (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of OE-CQDs were depicted in Fig. 6 A. It was seen that there is a partial spectrum overlap between the excitation spectra of OE-CQDs and the absorption band of NC, indicating that IFE was deemed to be a possible quenching mechanism (Yang et al., 2019a).

To explore if any other quenching mechanism coexists in the quenching system, time resolved fluorescence spectrometry was investigated. Generally, fluorescence quenchers can be classified as static quenching and dynamic quenching which can be distinguished from lifetime differences. The fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore would be reduced in the presence a dynamic quencher and keeps nearly constant in a static quenching process (Zu et al., 2017). Fig. 6B displayed the fluorescence decay curves of OE-CQDs and OE-CQDs/NC. The average fluorescence lifetime of OE-CQDs before and after mixing with NC (Table S2) were 1.25 and 1.11 ns, respectively. It can be seen that the introduction of NC did not cause obvious change of the fluorescence lifetime of OE-CQDs, implying that the fluorescence quenching of OE-CQDs by NC was due to a static interaction process (Gao et al., 2019). The zeta potential measurements also supported the static interaction between OE-CQDs and NC. As shown in Table S3, the zeta potential of pure OE-CQDs was 14.83 mV, indicating that the surface functionalities impart OE-CQDs with positive charges. The zeta potential of the OE-CQD/NC mixture was 13.7 mV, indicating that a strong electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interaction occurred between OE-CQD and NC (Gong et al., 2019). The change in the zeta potential of OE-CQDs caused by NC further confirmed the existence of static interactions between OE-CQDs and NC. In essence, the quenching mechanism of OE-CQDs by NC was a combined effect of IFE and static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NC contents in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the feasibility and accuracy of the proposed OE-CQDs probe for detecting NC content in practical application, the experiments were designed to determine NC contents in commercial food samples such as dried waxberry, haw flakes, watermelon frost lozenges, strawberry jam and energy drink. As shown in Table 2, the contents of NC in the above five food samples were determined to be 0.16, 0.24, 0.18, 0.21 and 0.15 µmol L−1, respectively. The spiked recoveries were between 97.0% and 104.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.74%, indicating that the proposed method was of high accuracy. In addition, the HPLC analyses were also performed. The typical chromatograms of NC standard and the extractions of above five food samples are depicted in Fig. S4. As shown in Table 2, the analysis results achieved by the HPLC method were in good consistence with that obtained by the proposed fluorescence method, which unambiguously demonstrate that our proposed method was highly accurate for the determination of NC in real samples.

In this work, an efficient and ultrasensitive fluorescence method was for the first time developed for the rapid determination of NC by using OE-CQDs nanoprobe with long-wavelength emission at 590 nm. The fluorescence emission of OE-CQDs can be significantly inhibited by NC via IFE and static interaction. The sensing platform displayed a linear relationship with the NC concentration in a range of 0.01–20.0 µmol L−1 and achieved a detection limit of 1.8 nmol L−1. The reported fluorescence method was used for the determination of NC in five commercial food samples with recoveries ranging from 97.0% to 104.0% and RSDs less than 3.74%. The proposed analytical method exhibited the advantages of good selectivity, high sensitivity, fast detection, and low cost. The results of this work showed the feasibility and dependability of using OE-CQDs as nanoprobe to quantify NC in complex food matrix.

Yikun Cui: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – revise manuscript & editing. Qin Hu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhen-quan Yang: Resources, Conceptualization, writing – review & editing. Lizhen Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Xiaojuan Gong: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Ming Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Lixia Xiao: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Jie Han: Resources, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:section.ce:para: All authors agree with this submission and declare that there are no competing of financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
